1: כָּל-הָעַמִּים תִּקְעוּ-כָף (kol-hāʿammîm tiqĕʿû-kāp, "Clap your hands, all you peoples") — the universal summons: not Israel alone but kol-hāʿammîm (all the peoples/nations — the full breadth of humanity). Tāqaʿ kap: to strike the palm (clapping as the physical expression of acclamation — used at the coronation of kings, 2 Kgs 11:12). הָרִיעוּ לֵאלֹהִים בְּקוֹל רִנָּה (hārîʿû lêʾlōhîm bĕqôl rinnāh, "shout to God with a voice of joy") — rûaʿ: to shout, to raise the war-cry/victory shout (the tĕrûʿāh of battle and celebration). Rinnāh: the ringing joy-cry.
2: כִּי-יְהוָה עֶלְיוֹן נוֹרָא (kî-YHWH ʿelyôn nôrāʾ, "For the LORD Most High is to be feared") — ʿElyôn: the Most High (the highest divine title, combining the covenantal name YHWH with the superlative divine appellative). Nôrāʾ: awesome, fearsome (from yārēʾ: to fear) — the cause of holy fear. מֶלֶךְ גָּדוֹל עַל-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (melek gādôl ʿal-kol-hāʾāreṣ, "he is a great King over the whole earth") — melek gādôl: the great king — the suzerain title used in Assyrian royal inscriptions. The psalm appropriates the language of ancient Near Eastern imperial lordship and applies it to YHWH over all the earth.
3: יַדְבֵּר עַמִּים תַּחְתֵּינוּ (yadĕbbêr ʿammîm taḥtênû, "He subdues peoples under us") — dābar (piel: to subdue, to bring into subjection — a rare piel usage). וּלְאֻמִּים תַּחַת רַגְלֵינוּ (ûlĕʾummîm taḥat raglênû, "and nations beneath our feet") — "under the feet" as the posture of the conquered (Ps 18:38-39; Josh 10:24). The nations placed beneath Israel by divine action.
4: יִבְחַר-לָנוּ אֶת-נַחֲלָתֵנוּ (yibḥar-lānû ʾet-naḥălātênû, "He chose our inheritance for us") — bāḥar: to choose (the election vocabulary of Deut 7:6 — as in Ps 33:12). Naḥălāh: inheritance, portion (the land as the covenantal inheritance). אֶת גְּאוֹן יַעֲקֹב אֲשֶׁר-אָהֵב (ʾet gĕʾôn yaʿăqōb ʾăsher-ʾāhêb, "the glory of Jacob whom he loved") — gĕʾôn yaʿăqōb: the "pride/majesty of Jacob" — the land or the people as the subject of divine love. Selāh.
5: עָלָה אֱלֹהִים בִּתְרוּעָה (ʿālāh ʾĕlōhîm bitĕrûʿāh, "God has ascended with shouts of joy") — ʿālāh: to go up, to ascend. The divine ascent — possibly the ascent of the Ark to Jerusalem (2 Sam 6), or the ascent of YHWH to his heavenly throne after the victory. Bitĕrûʿāh: "with the blast/shout" — the joyful military/coronation cry. Paul uses the vocabulary of divine ascent (from Ps 68:18, which shares the context of Ps 47) in Eph 4:8 for Christ's ascension. יְהוָה בְּקוֹל שׁוֹפָר (YHWH bĕqôl shôpār, "the LORD with the sound of a ram's horn") — shôpār: the ram's horn trumpet used at coronations, holy days, and divine appearances (Exod 19:13, 16, 19). The ascent announced by the royal trumpet of the divine King.
6: זַמְּרוּ אֱלֹהִים זַמֵּרוּ (zammĕrû ʾĕlōhîm zammêrû, "Sing praises to God, sing praises!") — the intensive repetition: zāmar (to sing with instruments) four times in vv6-7 — more concentrated than anywhere else in the Psalter. זַמְּרוּ לְמַלְכֵּנוּ זַמֵּרוּ (zammĕrû lĕmalkênû zammêrû, "Sing praises to our King, sing praises!") — the double ascription: God and King. The four-fold zammĕrû without content specifics — the praise itself without needing to enumerate what is praised.
7: כִּי מֶלֶךְ כָּל-הָאָרֶץ אֱלֹהִים (kî melek kol-hāʾāreṣ ʾĕlōhîm, "For God is the King of the whole earth") — the universal kingship declared as the reason for the universal praise. זַמְּרוּ מַשְׂכִּיל (zammĕrû maskîl, "sing praises with understanding") — maskîl: with understanding/wisdom (the same word as the psalm-type heading). The praise is not merely emotional or musical but intellectual — understanding what it is that is being praised. Intelligent praise.
8: מָלַךְ אֱלֹהִים עַל-גּוֹיִם (mālaḵ ʾĕlōhîm ʿal-gôyim, "God reigns over the nations") — mālaḵ: to reign, to be king. The present reality of divine kingship: not merely that God will reign but that he reigns now over the nations (gôyim) — including those who do not yet acknowledge it. אֱלֹהִים יָשַׁב עַל-כִּסֵּא קָדְשׁוֹ (ʾĕlōhîm yāshab ʿal-kissēʾ qodshô, "God is seated on his holy throne") — yāshab: to sit, to be enthroned (as in Ps 29:10: the LORD sat enthroned over the Flood). The holy throne (kissēʾ qodshô) as the seat of universal authority.
9: נְדִיבֵי עַמִּים נֶאֱסָפוּ (nĕdîbê ʿammîm neʾĕsāpû, "The nobles of the nations have gathered") — nādîb: the voluntary, freely-giving noble; the generous prince. The willing/voluntary gathering of the nations' nobility. עַם אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם (ʿam ʾĕlōhê ʾabrāhām, "as the people of the God of Abraham") — the most surprising claim: the nobles of the nations gathered as the people of the God of Abraham — not absorbed into Israel but joining the covenant community as full members. This is the eschatological fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise: "through you all the families of the earth will be blessed" (Gen 12:3). Paul's theology of Gentile inclusion (Gal 3:8: "the Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith") draws on exactly this horizon. כִּי לֵאלֹהִים מָגִנֵּי-אֶרֶץ מְאֹד נַעֲלָה (kî lêʾlōhîm māginnê-ʾereṣ mĕʾōd naʿălāh, "for the rulers of the earth belong to God; he is highly exalted") — the ultimate claim of the psalm: all earthly rulers, all shields/protectors of the earth, belong to God — mĕʾōd naʿălāh: "greatly exalted." The final word is the exaltation that began with the ascending God (v5).
